texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the attempt to reduce the greenhouse gas emissions in the fight against the climate change, the energy-intensive industries have to implement innovative low carbon technologies in the near future [1]. Improving the energy conversion efficiency is one crucial aspect but other potential solutions (e.g., application of carbon capture and storage methods) need also to be considered [2]. The iron and steel industry is one of the largest energy-consuming manufacturing sectors in the world accounting for about 10-15% of the total industrial primary energy consumption. The main energy source for the iron and steel production is based on fossil fuels (coal and natural gas). The CO2 emissions from production of one tonne of steel in an integrated steel mill have been estimated on average at 2 tonne CO2 [3].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reducing CO2 emissions from energy sector and other fossil fuel-intensive industrial applications is of main importance today. The iron and steel industry is one of the largest industrial sources of CO2 (about 6% of total CO2 emissions). Two post-combustion CO2 capture methods based on reactive gas-liquid and gas-solid systems are evaluated to be used in an integrated steel mill in conjunction with the plant sub-systems with the highest CO2 emissions e.g., captive power plant, hot stoves, coke ovens, lime kilns, etc. The gas-liquid absorption using chemical solvents (e.g., alkanolamines) and Calcium Looping (CaL) are assessed. The carbon capture rate is set to be at least 90%. The paper evaluates a conventional size of integrated steel mill emphasizing the energy integration aspects and the influence of various carbon capture options on the overall steel mill performances. The evaluated designs (captive power plants and carbon capture units) were modelled and simulated, the results being used to assess the overall indicators. For comparison reason, various captive power plant configurations of integrated steel mill without carbon capture were also considered. The assessments show that CaL system has significant advantages compared not only to benchmark cases without capture but also to the gas-liquid absorption cases.

texts.orig: Basically, there are two main routes for producing steel: the iron ore reduction using coke or natural gas and the steel scrap recycling. The main steel production process (accounting for about 70% globally) is based on iron ore reduction with coke through the Blast Furnace (BF) followed by an iron refining process in the Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF). This production method is known as integrated steel mill. The rest 30% of global steel production is based on Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) method which uses steel scrap, steel recycling and iron from a Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) processes. All iron and steel manufacturing steps are highly energy intensive leading to significant fossil CO2 emissions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iron and steel industry is heavily dependent on carbon-based fuels as reducing agents of iron ore; currently the carbon is coming mainly from fossil sources. CCS technologies have the potential to significantly reduce the greenhouse gas emissions and to permit the usage of fossil fuels as a bridge to future low carbon economy where the renewable will be the main energy source. The main CO2 capture options are: post-combustion, pre-combustion and oxycombustion [8,9]. Considering that an integrated steel mill has numerous CO2 emission sources (the most important being the captive power plant, hot stoves, lime kilns, coke ovens), the postcombustion capture configuration seems to be the obvious choice [10]. The most mature carbon capture option is based on postcombustion method using reactive gas-liquid absorption using various chemical solvents (e.g., Methyl-DiEthanol-Amine - MDEA). The main drawbacks of chemical gas-liquid absorption are high energy duty for solvent regeneration (  3 MJ/kg CO2) and operational aspects (solvent degradation, corrosion, etc.).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both CaL reactors are operating at high temperature: the carbonation reactor at about 500-600 ° C and the calcination reactor at about 900-980 ° C. The high operating temperature of CaL system enables the high pressure steam generation with positive influence on overall energetic management of the steel mill. In addition, the spent calcium-based sorbent can be easily utilised in the steel mill (e.g., sinter production, basic oxygen furnace, ladle metallurgy) reducing the output of lime kilns which is an important CO2 emitter.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This article evaluates a conventional size of integrated steel mill with 4 million tonnes Hot Rolled Coil (HRC) per year output with and without carbon capture. The steel mill design without carbon capture was considered as a benchmark case [3]. For the benchmark case (steel mill without carbon capture), various configurations were considered for the captive power plant to evaluate the potential CO2 emission reduction via power block retrofit. The assessed post-combustion CO2 capture options are based on reactive gas-liquid (MDEA) and gas-solid (CaL) configurations. Various carbon capture scenarios from the most important CO2 emission sources within the steel mill (e.g., captive power plant, hot stoves, lime plant, coke ovens) were considered. The energy integration aspects and the influence of various carbon capture options and operational scenarios on the overall techno-economic and environmental performances were assessed. The evaluated designs with and without carbon capture were simulated using process flow modelling, the simulation results being used to assess the overall technical and environmental indicators (e.g., energy efficiency, ancillary consumptions, carbon capture rate, specific CO2 emissions, CO2 capture investment costs).
texts.text: This article evaluates a conventional size of integrated steel mill with 4 million tonnes Hot Rolled Coil (HRC) per year output with and without carbon capture. The steel mill design without carbon capture was considered as a benchmark case [3]. For the benchmark case (steel mill without carbon capture), various configurations were considered for the captive power plant to evaluate the potential CO2 emission reduction via power block retrofit. The assessed post-combustion CO2 capture options are based on reactive gas-liquid (MDEA) and gas-solid (CaL) configurations. Various carbon capture scenarios from the most important CO2 emission sources within the steel mill (e.g., captive power plant, hot stoves, lime plant, coke ovens) were considered. The energy integration aspects and the influence of various carbon capture options and operational scenarios on the overall techno-economic and environmental performances were assessed. The evaluated designs with and without carbon capture were simulated using process flow modelling, the simulation results being used to assess the overall technical and environmental indicators (e.g., energy efficiency, ancillary consumptions, carbon capture rate, specific CO2 emissions, CO2 capture investment costs).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capturing process was concentrated on the most important effluents of the integrated steel mill. As an illustrative example, Table 1 presents the specific CO2 emissions per tonne of Hot Rolled Coil (HRC) for each sub-system of integrated steel mill [3]. As can be noticed, the most important CO2 emitters within an integrated steel mill are: captive power plant, hot stoves, sinter plant, coke oven and lime kilns. For the investigated carbon capture designs, two post-combustion carbon capture methods were evaluated. The first carbon capture concept is using an absorption - desorption cycle using an aqueous solution of MDEA [13]. The conceptual layout of reactive gas-liquid absorption process is presented in Fig. 2.

The lean solvent is recycled to the absorption column and the captured CO2 stream is then conditioned (compressed and dried) and send to the storage/utilisation sites. texts.orig: The second carbon capture concept is using the Calcium Looping (CaL) cycle. As presented above, the CO2 is captured (adsorbed) in the carbonator using a calcium-based sorbent (reaction 1). Then the sorbent is transferred in a separate reactor (calciner) where the regeneration process is done (reaction 2). Since the calcium carbonate decomposition is an endothermic process, additional fuel must be burnt in oxy-combustion conditions. As additional fuel used in the calciner, natural gas is mandatory to be used to limit the nitrogen contamination of the captured CO2 stream. Other steel mill combustible gases (e.g., COG, BFG and BOFG) have significant amounts of nitrogen which, if used in calcination reactor will end up in the captured CO2. The conceptual layout of reactive gas-liquid absorption process is presented in Fig. 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the carbonation -calcination cycle, a part of the calcium-based sorbent got deactivated due to various factors (e.g., attrition). To maintain the sorbent capacity to capture CO2, part of the solid inventory is extracted and replaced with fresh (make-up) sorbent. The spent material can then be easily integrated in the steel mill as lime in various steel production steps (e.g., sinter production, basic oxygen steelmaking, ladle metallurgy, etc.).

The most restrictive storage option, namely Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) was chosen in this analysis [14]. 120 bar as CO2 delivery pressure at plant gate was also considered in all cases.

texts.orig: The main design characteristics of the evaluated concepts are presented in Table 2 [3,10,12,15,16]. The evaluations were concentrated mainly on captive power plant options and carbon capture & conditioning units. For the rest of the steel mill sub-systems, available data from literature were used [3]. The following general assumptions for the integrated steel mills were considered: annual production of 4 million Hot Rolled Coil (HRC); captive ownership of lime kilns, air separation unit and power plant; no coke and electricity imports and exports are considered; natural gas was considered as auxiliary fuel for covering the energy duty only when internal steel mill off gases (COG, BFG, BOFG) are not enough.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All evaluated designs were modelled and simulated using ChemCAD software [17]. The simulation was based on thermodynamic equilibrium calculations. The simulation of the models provided the mass and energy balances needed to evaluate the main plant performances. The simulation results were compared with experimental/numerical data from literature to validate the models [3]. For energy optimisation, the evaluated captive power plants and the carbon capture options were thermally integrated using pinch method [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be noticed from Fig. 4, using a more efficient power block based on combined cycle gas turbine with duct firing can significantly reduce the fuel consumption and improve the overall energy efficiency. For integrated steel mill with carbon capture, the overall energy intensity is increasing due to the heat requirements for solvent/sorbent regeneration. The notable aspect here is that the chemical looping option has a significant high temperature recovery potential because of high operating temperatures of the carbonator and calcination reactors. In contrast, the gas-liquid absorption operates near ambient temperature and the heat recovery potential is negligible. Fig. 5 presents the composite curves for calcium looping unit used to decarbonise BFG and BOFG (Case 3A).

texts.orig: Table 3 presents the main performances of captive power plants without carbon capture. In all evaluated cases, the generated power is used only to drive the steel mill (no electricity imports/exports). The annual daily average power output in all cases was about 183 MWe (85% load was considered for Cases 1, 1A and 1B and 92% load was considered for Case 1C).
texts.text: Table 3 presents the main performances of captive power plants without carbon capture. In all evaluated cases, the generated power is used only to drive the steel mill (no electricity imports/exports). The annual daily average power output in all cases was about 183 MWe (85% load was considered for Cases 1, 1A and 1B and 92% load was considered for Case 1C).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The introduction of more efficient power blocks (boiler with increased steam pressure and steam reheat, combined cycle) implies an increase of the net power efficiency from 32% (base case) to about 42.5% (combined cycle with duct firing case). The specific CO2 emissions of the power plant (expressed as kg CO2/MW h) are reduced by almost 11% for Case 1C compared to the base case (Case 1). When evaluates the specific CO2 emissions at the whole steel mill level, the reduction is only marginal with about 2%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The conclusion is that only the retrofit of the captive power plant is not substantially reduce the overall steel mill carbon footprint. Carbon capture methods have to be integrated in the iron and steel industry to significantly reduce the CO2 emissions. The two post-combustion carbon capture methods (gas-liquid absorption and calcium looping) were evaluated in various steel mill decarbonisation scenarios.
texts.text: The conclusion is that only the retrofit of the captive power plant is not substantially reduce the overall steel mill carbon footprint. Carbon capture methods have to be integrated in the iron and steel industry to significantly reduce the CO2 emissions. The two post-combustion carbon capture methods (gas-liquid absorption and calcium looping) were evaluated in various steel mill decarbonisation scenarios.

texts.orig: steam plant and hot stoves (Case 2A); (ii) post-combustion CO2 capture from steam plant, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens. Both cases used a state of the art NGCC power block to generate the electricity to drive the whole steel mill (including the CO2 capture unit). The NGCC power plant does not have a carbon capture unit; CO2 is captured only from the steel mill flue gases (steam plant, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens). The steam plant used steel mill off gases (BFG and BOFG) to generate the steam required for solvent regeneration (reboiler of the CO2 desorption column). The steam parameters in the steam plant were 9 bar and 250 ° C. The calculated energy duty for solvent regeneration is about 2.95 MJ/kg CO2 which is consistent with experimental data from the literature [21].
texts.text: steam plant and hot stoves (Case 2A); (ii) post-combustion CO2 capture from steam plant, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens. Both cases used a state of the art NGCC power block to generate the electricity to drive the whole steel mill (including the CO2 capture unit). The NGCC power plant does not have a carbon capture unit; CO2 is captured only from the steel mill flue gases (steam plant, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens). The steam plant used steel mill off gases (BFG and BOFG) to generate the steam required for solvent regeneration (reboiler of the CO2 desorption column). The steam parameters in the steam plant were 9 bar and 250 ° C. The calculated energy duty for solvent regeneration is about 2.95 MJ/kg CO2 which is consistent with experimental data from the literature [21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 4 presents the main performances of two postcombustion capture cases using gas-liquid absorption. As can be observed the carbon capture rate in both cases is higher than 90%. The specific CO2 emissions for the overall steel mill are in the range of 833-1048 kg/t HRC (50-60% reduction in comparison to the base case without carbon capture). Considering that chemical gas-liquid absorption is a technologically and commercially mature process, this decarbonisation method can be applied in short to medium term to reduce by half the carbon intensity of iron and steel industry. Further reduction of carbon footprint for steel production can be achieved using innovative carbon capture methods like calcium looping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The second post-combustion carbon capture method was based on calcium looping. As for the absorption case, two decarbonisation scenarios were evaluated: (i) post-combustion CO2 capture from flue gases resulted from blast furnace gas and basic oxygen furnace gas (Case 3A); (ii) post-combustion CO2 capture from flue gases resulted from blast furnace gas, basic oxygen furnace gas, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens (Case 3B). Since both BFG and BOFG contain significant amount of nitrogen, these combustible gases cannot be used in the calcination reactor to provide the heat for sorbent regeneration. These combustible gases were used in a CCGT power block to generate the electricity to drive the whole steel mill (including the CO2 capture unit). The flue gases from CCGT power plant are then sent to the carbon capture unit (Case 3A). Case 3B considers CO2 capture from flue gases coming from the power plant (similar with Case 3A) and flue gases from hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens. The energy integration aspects as well as the potential integration of the spent sorbent (lime) within the steel plant are important factors to be considered for calcium looping cycle [22] and they were carefully assessed in this work (see the energy integration analysis).

As for the absorption case, two decarbonisation scenarios were evaluated: (i) post-combustion CO2 capture from flue gases resulted from blast furnace gas and basic oxygen furnace gas (Case 3A); (ii) post-combustion CO2 capture from flue gases resulted from blast furnace gas, basic oxygen furnace gas, hot stoves, lime kilns and coke ovens (Case 3B). texts.orig: Table 6 presents the estimation of capital costs for CO2 capture and conditioning units as well as specific investment costs. It must be stated that these capital and investment costs are only for the CO2 capture and conditioning units not for the whole steel mill. Capital and investment costs of conventional integrated steel mill without carbon capture are available in the literature [3,27,28]. The specific investment cost for an integrated steel mill without carbon capture is close to 1000 € /t HRC [3]. The introduction of carbon capture technologies within the iron and steel industry implies an increase of specific investment costs up to 12% for gas-liquid absorption and up to 6% for calcium looping. One can observe that the calcium looping shows significantly reduced investments than gas-liquid absorption (  50% lower).

texts.orig: As overall conclusion from the techno-economic results presented above, the calcium looping is a promising postcombustion CO2 capture option to deliver higher decarbonisation rate as well as increased energy efficiency and reduced capital cost investments compared to gas-liquid absorption. In addition, the spent sorbent can be integrated in the steel plant.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper evaluates the potential methods to reduce the carbon emissions from iron and steel industry. As an illustrative example, an integrated steel mill with 4 million tonnes hot rolled coil production per year was considered. Various CO2 reduction methods were assessed: from captive power plant retrofit to introduction of carbon capture within the steel mill. As evaluated post-combustion carbon capture methods chemical gas-liquid absorption (MDEA-based) and calcium looping were analysed. Key technical performances (e.g., fuel consumption, energy efficiency, gross/net power generation, specific CO2 emissions, etc.) and capital cost investment for CO2 capture and conditioning units were quantified.
texts.text: This paper evaluates the potential methods to reduce the carbon emissions from iron and steel industry. As an illustrative example, an integrated steel mill with 4 million tonnes hot rolled coil production per year was considered. Various CO2 reduction methods were assessed: from captive power plant retrofit to introduction of carbon capture within the steel mill. As evaluated post-combustion carbon capture methods chemical gas-liquid absorption (MDEA-based) and calcium looping were analysed. Key technical performances (e.g., fuel consumption, energy efficiency, gross/net power generation, specific CO2 emissions, etc.) and capital cost investment for CO2 capture and conditioning units were quantified.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As presented in the paper, the captive power plant retrofit to more efficient power generation systems (e.g., higher pressure boiler with steam reheat, combined cycle gas turbine with duct firing) produced modest results in term of reducing specific CO2 emissions per tonne of HRC at the whole steel mill level. A far more promising option to significantly reduce the CO2 emissions represents the implementation of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technologies. The two investigated CO2 capture options were assessed in various steel mill decarbonisation scenarios. CO2 capture was concentrated mostly to the main CO2 emitters e.g., blast furnace & basic oxygen furnace flue gases (about 50% overall steel mill decarbonisation degree) and blast furnace, basic oxygen furnace, lime kilns and coke oven flue gases (about 60-70% overall steel mill decarbonisation degree). A carbon capture rate higher than 90% was considered for both CO2 capture methods.

A far more promising option to significantly reduce the CO2 emissions represents the implementation of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technologies. The two investigated CO2 capture options were assessed in various steel mill decarbonisation scenarios. CO2 capture was concentrated mostly to the main CO2 emitters e.g., blast furnace & basic oxygen furnace flue gases (about 50% overall steel mill decarbonisation degree) and blast furnace, basic oxygen furnace, lime kilns and coke oven flue gases (about 60-70% overall steel mill decarbonisation degree). A carbon capture rate higher than 90% was considered for both CO2 capture methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results showed that calcium looping has significant advantages in comparison to the gas-liquid absorption option. For instance, the overall steel mill specific CO2 emissions can be reduced down (in case of the highest decarbonisation scenario) to about 565 kg/t HRC in comparison to 833 kg/t HRC for MDEA case and 2092 kg/t HRC for base case without carbon capture. The estimation of capital costs required for CO2 capture and conditioning showed also a consistent advantage for the calcium looping cases (about 50% lower specific investment costs than gas-liquid absorption).
